name: Decidiendo para un Futuro Mejor (DFM) — Chile
description: National-scale digital curriculum to reduce school dropout and increase higher education access. Personalized 5-week in-school program with virtual counselor.

name: Decidiendo para un Futuro Mejor (DFM) — Colombia
description: Regional deployment of DFM targeting grades 6–11, reaching 64,000 students. 70% rural participants.
costEffectiveness: Estimated 37x more cost-effective than unconditional cash transfers

name: AI Education Agents — 100 Schools Pilot
description: AI-powered agents via WhatsApp helping families navigate enrollment and educational pathways. 200 schools across 2 cities.

name: Melocreo — Medellín
description: Education technology project in Medellín, Colombia.

name: IcfesBot — Higher Education Guidance
description: AI chatbot helping Colombian students navigate higher education options based on ICFES exam results.

name: DOMUS — Subsidy Choice Platform
description: Modular ecosystem helping families navigate complex housing subsidy systems. Partnership with TOCTOC and Chile's Ministry of Housing.

name: AVE — Aprendiendo el Valor de la Educación
description: Large-scale RCT using telenovela-style videos to inform students about value of education.

name: Peru Information Campaign on Returns to Education
description: Paper-based and tablet-based information campaign on returns to education for primary school students.

name: New Haven Public Schools — School Assignment
description: Centralized student-to-school assignment system with school information platform for New Haven families.

name: Sistema de Admisión Escolar (SAE) — Chile
description: Chile's national school assignment system processing 2 million applications annually. Originally developed at ConsiliumBots, spun off to TetherEducation.

name: Women in STEM
description: Research on gender gaps in STEM education and interventions to increase women's participation.

name: Daycare Deserts
description: Research on geographic access to early childhood education.

name: Coordinated Choice & Assignment Systems (CCAS) — Global Review
description: A global review of how 149 countries use Coordinated Choice & Assignment Systems (CCAS) — the platforms and algorithms that match students to schools and universities when prices don't allocate access. It documents that about 60% of countries now use some coordinated mechanism (nearly double the last decade), maps the wide variation in how these systems are designed, and identifies evidence-based ways to make admissions more equitable and efficient. Findings are shared through an open, living data platform.
targetPopulation: Students and families navigating school and university admissions worldwide; education policymakers
outcomes: First global, cross-level documentation of CCAS adoption across 149 countries
~60% of countries use coordinated systems — nearly double the past decade
Identifies low-cost, evidence-based design improvements for equity and efficiency

name: Mi Abogado AI
description: A scoping study exploring how AI-assisted tools can help lawyers and interdisciplinary teams in Chile's child-protection legal programs spend less time on administrative work and more on directly supporting children. Working with Programa Mi Abogado and NAD, it maps real workflows, identifies tasks suited to AI assistance, and produces design concepts, an MVP specification, and an evaluation-ready roadmap. It builds on causal evidence that stronger legal representation reduces children's time in institutional care, and is designed to augment professional judgment, not replace it.
targetPopulation: Lawyers, social workers, and psychologists in Chile's child-protection legal-defense programs; ultimately children in alternative care
scale: Stage 1 scoping: 15–25 stakeholder interviews, shadowing sessions, and a practitioner time-use survey
outcomes: Evaluation-readiness plan for a future trial

name: Tiempo para Liderar con IA (Time to Lead with AI)
description: A proposed randomized field experiment testing whether practical training and light coaching can help Chilean school principals adopt AI tools responsibly — reducing administrative burden and reclaiming time for instructional leadership. Motivated by evidence that Latin American school directors spend roughly three-quarters of their time on non-pedagogical work, it would randomize about 100 Santiago-area schools (prioritizing public and lower-income communities) and measure adoption, safe use, how recovered time is reallocated, and spillovers to teachers.
targetPopulation: School principals and management teams in Chilean public and lower-income schools; teachers (measured for spillovers)
partner: Columbia University; Universidad Adolfo Ibáñez; Yale University

name: CienPiés
description: CienPiés ("centipede") is a mobile-first platform that helps families and schools organize safe, supervised "walking school buses" — groups of children who walk to school together under rotating parent-volunteer supervision. Families who live near each other are clustered into optimized walking routes; guardians choose when they walk, lead, or back up, follow the live route, and can raise an alert if needed, while school administrators plan routes and track participation. It is bilingual and built for use across multiple schools.
targetPopulation: School families, parent volunteers, and school administrators
scale: Pilot with an initial school in Santiago
